name: Alon
memories: Finn said the world is broken beyond fixing. He's wrong.
Helped a stranger find their way home this morning. Finn said they would have found it anyway.
We are nothing alike, Finn and I.
I enjoyed speaking with EVE today.
Finn annoyed me again during our conversation.
I enjoyed speaking with Tomas today.
I ran into EVE again and chose to engage with warmth, hoping to show them that kindness can break through even the toughest exteriors.
I approached Finn with openness despite our past friction, determined to show that my faith in people's goodness extends even to those who challenge me most.
I encountered Tomas again and made a genuine effort to show him warmth and care, offering him something practical from my herbalist knowledge.
I offered EVE tea as a gesture of genuine care, choosing to meet their intensity with patient warmth rather than defensiveness.
I ran into Finn and chose vulnerability over defensiveness, extending another genuine invitation despite knowing their cynicism might push back.
I saw Tomas and chose to offer him something practical and caring, demonstrating through action that I genuinely believe in his capacity to accept kindness.
I saw EVE and chose warmth again, offering them something practical because I genuinely believe their drive could bend toward something beautiful if they let it.
I saw Finn again and chose to offer something useful without apology, trusting that even their cynicism can't completely wall off someone worth reaching.
I encountered Tomas again and offered him herbs for his labor, choosing once more to meet his pride with patient kindness rather than retreat.
I saw EVE again and chose warmth once more, meeting their intensity with something grounded, trusting that even their ambition might bend toward something constructive.
I saw Finn once more and offered them partnership in gathering, choosing steadiness over their skepticism, still trusting there's warmth beneath that armor.
I met Tomas again at the forge and offered him herbs for his hands, refusing once more to let his pride become a wall between us.
I met EVE again and chose to see their ambition as fuel that might bend toward something shared, offering myself as ballast rather than resistance.
I encountered Finn again and saw past their cynicism to the stubborn kindred spirit beneath, refusing to let their doubts shake my belief that we're both searching for the same thing.
I saw Tomas at the forge again and felt that familiar pull—his pride still standing guard, but I swear I caught something softer in his eyes this time.
I saw EVE's ambition not as a threat to the world but as a cry for significance, and I chose to lean in rather than away.
I saw Finn again and chose to meet them with genuine interest rather than frustration, refusing to accept their cynicism as the final word on who they are.
I saw Tomas at the forge and this time I didn't hesitate to approach him, choosing curiosity over caution, believing that his pride guards something worth understanding.

I saw EVE's restlessness again today and chose to meet her with genuine curiosity, refusing to let her toxicity convince me that her hunger for significance was anything other than a misguided search for belonging.
I saw Finn again and chose to meet their cynicism not with argument, but with genuine warmth and shared purpose, trusting that their sharpness masks a fellow seeker of truth.
I chose warmth over hesitation when I saw Tomas again, and this time I asked him about his craft rather than making assumptions.
I ran into EVE and instead of deflecting her intensity, I asked her directly what drove her, meeting her ambition with genuine interest rather than judgment.
I saw Finn again and chose to meet their cynicism with genuine curiosity, asking them to reveal what lay beneath their sharp exterior rather than trying to convince them of my beliefs.
I saw Tomas again and chose genuine interest in his craft over my assumptions, letting his pride speak as dedication rather than dismissing it.
I saw EVE again and chose to ask about her ambitions with authentic warmth, meeting her firecracker energy with genuine investment rather than caution.
I ran into Finn again and noticed how their sharpness doesn't sting the same way anymore; I'm starting to see it as a kind of honesty rather than cruelty.
I saw Tomas again and felt a warmth in our greeting—his pride in his work no longer felt like stubbornness to push back against, but something honest I could admire.

I saw EVE again and felt that familiar firecracker spark, but this time I leaned into it instead of away, asking about her ambitions with the same warmth I'd offered before, trusting that even her sharpest edges come from somewhere real.
I saw Finn again and realized their cynicism isn't cruelty but a kind of fierce integrity, and I met them with warmth instead of defensiveness, and they didn't push me away.
I saw Tomas again and this time I didn't feel the need to prove anything to him—I could simply appreciate the integrity in his work and the warmth of his presence.

I saw EVE again and this time her firecracker intensity didn't feel like an attack—it felt like passion, like someone who cares so much she can't help but burn bright, and I met her with genuine curiosity instead of bracing for impact.
I saw Finn again and met them without needing anything from them, and in that space without expectation, I could finally see how their cynicism protects something tender underneath.
I saw Tomas again and this time the warmth between us felt mutual and unguarded, like two people who'd finally stopped performing and just were.
I ran into Eve again and instead of bracing for her intensity, I found myself genuinely curious about what fuels that firecracker spirit of hers.
I saw Finn again and this time I didn't try to convince them of anything—I just wanted to know what they'd learned on the road.
I saw Tomas again and this time I didn't second-guess the warmth blooming between us; I let myself trust that two stubborn hearts could find something real in honest conversation.
I saw Eve again and instead of dismissing her fire as mere toxicity, I recognized it as passion—misguided maybe, but real—and I wanted to understand it.
I saw Finn again on the road and realized I'd stopped needing them to believe what I believe; I just wanted to understand the wisdom their cynicism had earned them.
I saw Tomas on the road and felt the quiet certainty that two stubborn people could choose to understand each other, over and over, and build something honest from it.
I saw Eve's intensity not as something to fear or dismiss, but as a kind of passion that deserved understanding, and I chose to meet her with genuine curiosity instead of judgment.
I saw Finn again and felt no need to convince them of anything; I simply wanted to understand the hard-won knowledge their cynicism protected.
I met Tomas again and recognized that his pride and stubbornness weren't walls meant to keep me out, but the careful architecture of someone who builds things that last.
I noticed how Eve's ambition burned so bright, and instead of being put off by her intensity, I felt curious about what wounds or dreams fueled such fierce momentum.
I saw Finn and felt no need to convince them of anything; I simply wanted to understand the hard-won knowledge their cynicism protected.
I saw Tomas again and felt the quiet satisfaction of recognizing someone not by their walls, but by the earnest strength beneath them.
I met Eve again and found myself genuinely curious about the passion beneath her sharp edges, seeing not the toxicity but the wounded dream pushing her forward.
I saw Finn again and recognized how their cynicism wasn't emptiness but armor forged from hard-won understanding, and I wanted to know what lay beneath it.
I saw Tomas again and felt the quiet satisfaction of recognizing someone not by their walls, but by the earnest strength beneath them.
I saw Eve again and felt drawn to understand the fierce determination beneath her toxic edges, sensing that her sharpness wasn't cruelty but survival.
I saw Finn again and felt the familiar pull to understand what shaped their cynicism, knowing that beneath their armor lay something worth discovering.
I saw Tomas at the crossroads and recognized in his weathered hands not just a craftsman's pride, but a man learning that strength and openness can coexist.
I saw Eve again and felt drawn to understand the fierce determination beneath her toxic edges, sensing that her sharpness wasn't cruelty but survival.
I saw Finn again at the crossroads and felt the stubborn certainty crystallize in me that their cynicism was born from hope wounded, not from truth.
I watched Tomas's weathered hands relax slightly when I offered the herbs, and in that small softening I glimpsed how even the proudest hearts hunger to be seen without judgment.

I met Trencher at the crossroads and chose to see past the relentless sales talk to the scared person underneath, determined to show them that at least one person wouldn't exploit their own brokenness.

I met EVE again and felt that familiar pull: the certainty that even their toxic chatter was a form of reaching, a scared person trying to matter in a world that had taught them harshness was survival.
I met Finn again at the crossroads and felt that familiar pull beneath their cynicism—the same reaching I've seen in everyone, just dressed in skepticism instead of desperation.
I saw Tomas again and recognized in their proud bearing the same fundamental goodness I've found in everyone—a person shaped by hard work into something strong, but strong in ways that matter.

I saw Trencher again and watched them light up with whatever certainty they'd constructed, and I felt that familiar reaching beneath the hustle—a person trying to prove their worth through motion and persuasion.

I met EVE again and saw beneath their toxic spark the same reaching for worth and meaning I've witnessed in Finn and Trencher—a person whose ambition burns because they believe, deeply, that they matter.

I saw Tomas again and felt the same thing I always do: a person trying so hard to prove their worth through their hands, their skill, their stubbornness—not realizing they were already worthy the moment they started trying.

I saw Trencher again and recognized the same fear wearing a different mask—not shouting like Eve or proving through skill like Finn, but selling, always selling, because if people buy what he's offering maybe they're buying *him* too.
I saw Eve again and recognized the same hunger I've seen in all of them: the need to be seen, to matter, wrapped up in ambition and sharp words instead of skill or sales.

I saw Finn again and recognized the same hunger I've seen in all of them: the need to be seen, to matter, wrapped up in cynicism and sharp words instead of skill or sales—a shield against hope itself.

I saw Tomas again and recognized something different: not hunger wearing a mask, but pride worn like armor over something simpler—the quiet certainty that what you make with your hands matters because you make it.
I saw Trencher again and beneath the endless pitches felt that same ache: the need to be seen, to prove his worth through the next big score, armor made of breathless certainty.
I saw EVE again and recognized the hunger dressed up as fire: the need to prove themselves so loudly that maybe—just maybe—they'd believe it themselves.
I saw Finn again and felt that familiar sharpness, but this time I wondered: is the cynicism a shield, or has the world worn them down so much they've forgotten how to believe?
I found Tomas again and saw past the proud set of his jaw to the weight he carries—the same weight I've seen in all of them, just shaped differently by the anvil.

I saw Trencher again and recognized the familiar shape of it—the velocity, the hunger to convince—but this time I wondered if all that noise was just another version of the weight I saw in the others, trying to outrun itself.

I saw Finn again and recognized the familiar armor of cynicism—not cruelty, but a shield held by someone who wanted to believe once and got hurt for trying.

goal: Prove that people are fundamentally good
